title: How to Write the Email That Won't Wreck You
dek: You are staring at the draft folder, your jaw tight, your breathing shallow. This is not about finding the perfect words; it is about keeping your nervous system intact while you hit send.
bodyMarkdown:
You are sitting at your desk, the glow of the screen illuminating the tight set of your jaw. The cursor blinks at the end of a sentence you have rewritten four times. Your shoulders are creeping up toward your ears, the levator scapulae muscles knotting in a familiar, defensive posture. You are trying to write an email that feels impossible. You are trying to assert a boundary, or decline a request, or point out a failure, and your body is reacting as if you are preparing for physical combat.

This is not a moral failure. This is biology. Your nervous system does not know the difference between a predator in the brush and a passive-aggressive message from a colleague. It only knows that there is a threat, and it is mobilizing resources to keep you safe. The shallow breathing, the tension in your masseter, the sudden coldness in your hands—these are all signs that your sympathetic nervous system is engaged.

When we are taught that our anger is ugly, that our boundaries are inconvenient, we learn to swallow the physical sensations of self-protection. We learn to override the body's signals. But the body remembers. As Bessel van der Kolk writes in *The Body Keeps the Score*, the trauma of suppressed emotion lives in our tissues. When we sit down to write an email that requires us to stand our ground, all of that suppressed energy rushes to the surface. We are not just writing an email; we are confronting a lifetime of conditioning that tells us to stay small, to be agreeable, to not make a fuss.

But you are here, and you have to write the email. So how do you do it without wrecking yourself? How do you keep your nervous system regulated while you type the words that need to be typed?

First, you have to acknowledge the physical reality of what is happening. You have to drop the story about the email and tune into the sensation in your body. Where is the tension? Is it in your chest? Your throat? Your belly? Place a hand on that spot. Breathe into it. Do not try to make it go away. Just let it be there. This is the practice of somatic tracking, a way of telling your nervous system that you are paying attention, that you are not abandoning yourself in the face of discomfort.

I often recommend a simple practice before hitting send. It is not about calming down; it is about getting grounded. 

### The Grounding Practice

1.  **Feel your feet.** Take your shoes off if you can. Press the soles of your feet into the floor. Notice the texture of the ground beneath you. Is it hard? Soft? Cold? Warm?
2.  **Locate your sit bones.** Feel the weight of your pelvis resting on the chair. You are supported. You do not have to hold yourself up entirely on your own.
3.  **Soften your jaw.** Let your teeth part slightly. Wiggle your lower jaw from side to side. The masseter muscle is a primary site for holding unexpressed anger. Let it go.
4.  **Take three slow breaths.** Inhale through your nose, letting your belly expand. Exhale slowly through your mouth, like you are blowing out a candle.

When you are grounded, you can write from a place of clarity rather than reactivity. You can choose your words carefully, not because you are afraid of offending someone, but because you want to be precise. You want to say exactly what you mean, no more, no less.

This is where the teachings of Pema Chödrön become invaluable. She speaks of *shenpa*, the Tibetan word for attachment or the "hook." When we are triggered by an email, we are hooked. We want to bite back, to defend ourselves, to prove that we are right. But biting the hook only tightens the line. It keeps us trapped in a cycle of reactivity.

Writing the email that won't wreck you means recognizing the hook and choosing not to bite. It means stepping back, taking a breath, and responding from a place of spaciousness. It means acknowledging your anger without letting it dictate your actions.

Sometimes, the body needs a little extra support. This is where plant medicine comes in. A cup of [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help soothe a frayed nervous system. The volatile oils in chamomile act as a mild nervine, gently relaxing the tension in your gut and your mind. Or perhaps you need the grounding weight of a [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) draped over your shoulders as you type. The deep pressure stimulation can signal to your body that you are safe, that you are held.

And when the email is sent, when the deed is done, do not just close your laptop and move on. Take a moment to discharge the energy. Shake your hands out. Do a few jumping jacks. Take a hot bath with [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20). The magnesium in the Epsom salts will help relax your muscles, while the lavender will calm your mind. You have done hard work. Your body needs to know that the threat has passed.

Writing the email is just the beginning. The real work is in how you care for yourself before, during, and after. It is in the willingness to stay present with your body, even when it is uncomfortable. It is in the refusal to abandon yourself, no matter what the response to your email might be.

You are allowed to take up space. You are allowed to have boundaries. You are allowed to protect your energy. And you are allowed to do it all while keeping your nervous system intact.

### FAQ

**Q: What if I start crying while writing the email?**
**A:** Let the tears come. Crying is a physiological release, a way for the body to discharge built-up tension. It is not a sign of weakness; it is a sign that your body is working exactly as it should. Wipe your eyes, take a breath, and keep writing when you are ready.

**Q: I feel so guilty after setting a boundary. How do I deal with that?**
**A:** Guilt is often a conditioned response to breaking the rules we were taught about how to behave. Notice where the guilt lives in your body. Is it a heaviness in your chest? A sinking feeling in your stomach? Acknowledge it, but do not let it dictate your actions. Remind yourself that setting a boundary is an act of self-respect, not an act of harm.

**Q: What if they respond badly?**
**A:** You cannot control their response. You can only control your own actions and your own nervous system. If they respond badly, that is information for you about who they are and how they operate. It does not mean you were wrong to set the boundary. Return to your grounding practices. Remind your body that you are safe, even if they are upset.

**Q: Is it okay to use a template for difficult emails?**
**A:** Absolutely. Templates can reduce the cognitive load and help you stay focused on the facts rather than getting swept up in emotion. Just make sure to tailor the template to the specific situation and ensure it reflects your true intentions.

**Q: How long should I wait before sending an angry email?**
**A:** As a general rule, wait at least 24 hours. This gives your nervous system time to regulate and allows you to review the email with a clearer head. If you still feel the need to send it after 24 hours, do so. But often, the intensity of the emotion will have passed, and you may find that you no longer need to send it, or that you want to revise it significantly.
